plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones who are the highest beings in the world, its protectors, its well-wishers, its lamp, and its light.

wordByWord.gloss: to those supreme in the world
wordByWord.etymology: loga (< Skt. loka, 'world') + uttama (superlative, 'highest, most excellent') + -āṇaṁ (Prakrit genitive/dative plural of homage, < Skt. -ānām)
wordByWord.notes: Opens the 'loga' anaphora; the supremacy is now ranked within the whole inhabited cosmos, not merely among men (the previous 'purisa' cluster).

wordByWord.gloss: to the lords/protectors of the world
wordByWord.etymology: loga + nāha (< Skt. nātha, 'lord, protector, refuge', from √nāth) + -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: nātha here is guide-and-refuge, not a creator-deity; the Jina protects by showing the path, not by ruling.

wordByWord.gloss: to the benefactors of the world
wordByWord.etymology: loga + hia (< Skt. hita, 'benefit, welfare', past participle of √dhā) + -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: Marks the turn from status (uttama) and relationship (nātha) to active good done for others (hita).

wordByWord.gloss: to the lamps of the world
wordByWord.etymology: loga + païva (< Skt. pradīpa, 'lamp', from pra + √dīp, 'to blaze, shine') + -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: The Jina is himself the lamp — a self-luminous point of light amid the world's darkness of ignorance.
First printed segment of the single compound loga-pajjoyagarāṇaṁ, which the book sets with word-internal spacing (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: pajjoya (< Skt. pradyota, 'effulgence, blazing light', from pra + √dyut, 'to shine forth')
wordByWord.notes: Printed split as 'પજ્જો અગરાણં'; joins the following token to form pajjoyagara, 'light-maker'.

wordByWord.etymology: -gara (< Skt. -kara, 'maker, doer', from √kṛ) + -āṇaṁ; the whole = loga-pajjoyagarāṇaṁ, 'to the illuminators of the world'
wordByWord.notes: Where païva is a static lamp, pajjoyagara is the agent who actively floods the world with light — an intensifying escalation.

literalTranslation: To the supreme-in-the-world ones, to the lords of the world, to the benefactors of the world, to the lamps of the world, to the illuminators of the world.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence to the awakened ones: highest of all beings in the world, its true protectors and refuge, the well-wishers who work only for its good — the very lamp set against its darkness, and the light that makes the whole world shine.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Five honour-titles, all bound by the opening word loga ('world'), and they climb in a deliberate order. First comes rank — loguttama, highest of every being in the cosmos; then relationship — logaṇāha, the world's lord-and-refuge, where 'lord' means guide and shelter, not sovereign or maker. The third title, logahia, pivots from what the Jina is to what the Jina does: he is the world's benefactor, acting purely for its welfare. The last two titles seal the clause with the verse's governing image of light, and they are not a redundant pair. Loga-païva (pradīpa) names the Jina as a lamp — a self-luminous source standing in the dark. Loga-pajjoyagara (pradyota-kara) names him as the active illuminator who makes the world itself blaze with light. The movement is from being light to giving light, from the lamp on the stand to the radiance that floods the room. Read against the neighbouring clauses, this verse widens the lens: the preceding 'purisa' titles crowned the Jina first among men; here that supremacy is restated at cosmic scale (loga); and the verse that follows turns the abstract benefaction (hia) into concrete gifts — fearlessness, sight, path, refuge, awakening. This is the hinge between 'best of beings' and 'giver to beings.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The light-imagery is doctrinally exact, not merely poetic. In Jain epistemology the Arihanta's kevala-jñāna is omniscient knowing that dispels ajñāna-andhakāra, the darkness of ignorance; calling him lamp and illuminator asserts that he removes not the world's physical dark but its delusion (mithyātva). 'World' (loka) is the technical Jain cosmos of inhabited space filled with the six substances — so loka-uttama places the Jina above every class of soul in that cosmos, not just humanity. Crucially, logaṇāha must be read through Jain non-theism: the Jina is 'lord' as exemplar and refuge, never as creator or controller of the loka. And logahia grounds the titles in karuṇā/anukampā — the benefaction is the founding of the tīrtha and the teaching, the disinterested good a fully-detached being still works for all.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this clause, the practitioner stops admiring the Jina's greatness and starts relating to it: if he is the lamp, then my task is to turn toward the light and let it expose my own ignorance. The escalation lamp → illuminator is an invitation — not merely to behold a distant flame but to let that radiance actually illumine one's conduct and view today.

sources.title: Jain Quantum — Jain eLibrary literature search engine
sources.consultedFor: Attestation and English renderings of the Namutthuṇaṁ 'loga' clause (loguttamāṇaṁ … logapajjoyagarāṇaṁ) across indexed Āgama translations.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary — Panch Pratikraman and Āgama texts
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sūtra text and standard glosses of the five loga-titles.

notes: The book prints the final compound with word-internal spacing as 'લોગ પજ્જો અગરાણં'; this is preserved verbatim and the three printed segments together render the single Prakrit word loga-pajjoyagarāṇaṁ ('illuminators of the world'). The repeated ending -āṇaṁ is the Prakrit genitive/dative plural carrying the implied homage ('namo ... to those who are ...'). païva reflects pradīpa; some editions read padīvāṇaṁ — a spelling, not a sense, variant. pajjoyagara reflects pradyota-kara (light-maker); a minority of readings gloss it as udyota-kara (uddyotakara), with the same 'illuminator' sense.
